metadata.title: Polynomial Factoring — The Decision Tree That Replaces Guessing

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Ciao, Liam — in for Bear. Polynomial factoring — the decision tree that replaces guessing? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Polynomial factoring — the decision tree that replaces guessing?

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Polynomial factoring — the decision tree that replaces guessing.

lines: The decision tree that replaces guessing

beats.narration_text: Most students factor polynomials by trial and error. That works when the numbers are small and the instinct is trained. But there is a systematic decision tree that works every time — and Claude can build it as an animated visual you can follow step by step.
claude — write a Manim scene showing the polynomial factoring decision tree: check GCF, count terms, if 2 terms check difference of squares, if 3 terms use ac method or simple trinomial strategy

props.command:
claude "write a Manim scene showing the polynomial
  factoring decision tree:
  GCF → count terms → if 2: diff of squares
  → if 3: ac method or trinomial strategy"

beats.narration_text: The CLI writes it. factoring_tree.py — a function that takes the number of polynomial terms and returns the three-step strategy: extract GCF, count terms, then branch to difference of squares for two terms or find p and q for trinomials.

Run it. The decision tree appears top to bottom. Step 1: extract GCF. Arrow down to Step 2: how many terms? Branch left for 2 terms to difference-of-squares box. Branch right for 3 terms to trinomial box with the factor result x plus p times x plus q.
claude — add a worked example tracing x squared plus 5x plus 6 through the tree step by step

props.command:
claude "add a worked example tracing
  x^2+5x+6 through the tree step by step"

beats.narration_text: Six steps appear in sequence. GCF: none. Three terms: trinomial strategy. a equals 1, so find p and q where p times q equals 6 and p plus q equals 5. Pairs: 1 times 6 sums to 7 — no. 2 times 3 sums to 5 — yes. Answer: x plus 2 times x plus 3. Check: expands back to x squared plus 5x plus 6.

The decision tree makes the implicit explicit. Every factoring problem starts the same way: extract the GCF. Then the number of terms determines the strategy. Memorizing the tree takes thirty seconds; the tree then handles every case you will encounter in college algebra.

Take a polynomial with four terms and apply the grouping strategy — factor the first two and last two separately, then factor out the common binomial. Work three examples until the pattern is automatic.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact

Take a polynomial with four terms and apply the grouping strategy — factor the first two and last two separately, then factor out the common binomial. beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Polynomial Factoring — The Decision Tree That Replaces Guessing] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
